headline: Two Tankers Struck Near Hormuz
deck: Commercial fire returned to the strait on Monday night, nineteen days after Iran pledged toll-free safe passage in Islamabad. A Qatari LNG carrier and a Saudi crude tanker took damage within hours as Washington warned retaliation is likely and Brent crude ticked higher.

art.title: Monday Night Strikes East of Limah
art.caption: The Al Rekayyat reported fire eight nautical miles east of Limah while southbound through the Hormuz corridor; a second tanker was damaged hours later off the Omani coast · Map: Sprockett, Escalation Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1

body: At 2119 UTC on Monday, a southbound tanker eight nautical miles east of Limah, Oman, reported being struck on the port side by an unknown projectile that set a fire aboard, with no casualties logged in the initial warning [E1]. The vessel was the Al Rekayyat, IMO 9397339, a Nakilat-owned Qatari LNG carrier and the first direct strike on a Qatari gas ship of the war [E2][E5]. Crew distress traffic reported a port-side hit and an engine-room fire while confirming the crew remained safe [E5]. British military shipping monitors classified the weapon as an unknown projectile and named no launcher [E1].

Hours after the LNG carrier's alarm, maritime-security sources said a Saudi-flagged crude tanker believed to be the Wedyan suffered damage near the Omani coast [E6]. The cause of that second blast remained unknown [E6]. Regulators counted the Al Rekayyat episode as the fiftieth confirmed incident in the Hormuz corridor on the International Maritime Organization's highlighted-incidents register [E2]. Regional reporting tallied two commercial ships struck in the same waterway overnight [E7].

Two American officials said Iran's military fired at least two missiles at commercial ships on Monday night, leaving both vessels significantly damaged without reported casualties [E3]. Those officials judged American retaliation as likely in the aftermath [E3]. The weapon label split across the night: monitors logged an unknown projectile [E1], distress traffic described a port-side strike and engine-room fire [E5], and the American officials pointed to missiles [E3]. A separate one-week attack-halt agreement had expired before the strikes resumed, an assertion Axios attributed solely to its own official sources [E3].

Iranian state television said the LNG tanker came under attack after ignoring warnings, without directly claiming responsibility for the assault [E4]. The broadcast framed compliance with maritime instructions as the dividing line between safe passage and fire [E4]. The sequence arrived less than three weeks after diplomats signed a memorandum meant to keep commercial traffic moving through the chokepoint [E7][E8]. Nineteen days had passed since that Islamabad pledge when gunfire returned to waters the document had promised would stay open [E8][E1].

On 17 June, a fourteen-point memorandum obliged Iran to provide safe passage without charge for sixty days while a joint Iran-Oman committee designs the strait's future administration and services [E8]. In the weeks since, Iran's Revolutionary Guard Navy declared that only Iran-designated channels counted as safe routes and warned that movement outside them was highly dangerous and forbidden [E9]. Oman's foreign ministry answered publicly that navigation through the temporary corridor must remain free without the imposition of transit fees [E10]. The documented demands sketch a toll booth in plain sight even as the memorandum's free window runs toward a mid-August lapse [E8][E9][E10].

Brent crude rose sixty-one cents to $72.60 by 1140 GMT as the vessel attacks shifted energy traders' focus back toward supply risk [E11]. Saxo Bank strategist Ole Hansen placed the next escalation price thresholds at $75 and then $80 [E11]. Oil had already been climbing on recovery hopes before Monday night's hull damage added a war premium back into the barrel [E11]. Markets priced the strikes as negotiation with a fuse attached, not a closed waterway [E11][E8].

Iran's documented demands since the memorandum have built a price list at the chokepoint: designated lanes, passage rules, and a sixty-day free window that lapses toward mid-August [E8][E9]. Monday night's violence landed inside that ledger, with two commercial hulls carrying the cost of disputed passage terms [E1][E6][E7]. Monitors, crews, and American officials disagreed on the weapon label, yet each account pointed to the same invoice [E1][E3][E5]. Iran is not closing the Strait of Hormuz; it is invoicing it, and the munition is the reminder that the bill exists [E8][E9].

key_numbers.label: Days since Iran signed the MoU
Tolls due when the free window ends
